transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Lily, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess I’ve been thinking a lot about some things that happened recently. There was this party I went to with Ethan, and it got pretty chaotic. I just felt really overwhelmed by everything. I don’t know if it’s worth diving into, though. It’s just... a lot.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the party with Ethan was a pretty intense experience for you, and it left you feeling overwhelmed. It’s completely okay if you’re not sure about diving into it all right now. What part of that experience feels most significant to you at the moment?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think what stands out the most is just how out of control everything felt. Ethan was acting really erratically, and I couldn’t help but feel responsible for it, like I should have done something to help him. I guess that made me really anxious, and I ended up just drinking more than I should have. It’s like I was trying to cope, but it didn’t really help.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you felt a strong sense of responsibility for Ethan's behavior, which contributed to your anxiety during the party. It’s understandable that you turned to drinking as a way to cope with those overwhelming feelings. How did that experience of feeling out of control and anxious affect you afterwards?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Afterwards, I felt pretty drained and, honestly, a bit ashamed. I kept replaying the night in my head, thinking about what I could have done differently. It’s like I was stuck in this loop of self-blame. I didn’t want to talk about it with anyone because I felt embarrassed. I just wanted to forget it, but it kept nagging at me, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the aftermath of the party was really tough for you. Feeling drained and ashamed, along with that loop of self-blame, can be really heavy to carry. It makes sense that you’d want to forget about it, yet it keeps coming back to your mind. What do you think it is about that experience that feels so hard to let go of?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s just that feeling of helplessness. I wanted to be there for Ethan, but instead, I felt like I just made things worse. It’s hard to shake off the thought that I could have done something to help him, and now I’m left feeling like I failed. I guess it makes me question if I’m really good at handling these kinds of situations. It’s just... frustrating.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That feeling of helplessness really seems to weigh on you. It’s understandable to question your ability to handle similar situations when you feel like you didn’t meet your own expectations. It sounds frustrating to feel both responsible for Ethan and also disappointed in how you handled it. How do you usually cope with those feelings of frustration and self-doubt when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I usually try to distract myself, like by hanging out with friends or just keeping busy. Sometimes I’ll just binge-watch shows or go out, but it doesn’t really solve anything. I guess I’m just trying to avoid facing those feelings directly. It’s easier to push them aside than to deal with them head-on, you know? But then they just come back, and it feels like I’m stuck in this cycle.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like keeping busy and distracting yourself is a way to cope with those difficult feelings, even if it doesn’t provide a lasting solution. It’s completely understandable to want to avoid facing those emotions directly; they can be really overwhelming. Yet, it seems like this approach leaves you feeling stuck in a cycle. What do you think it would be like to explore those feelings more directly, even just a little?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess exploring those feelings directly sounds... kind of scary. I mean, I worry that if I really dive into them, it might just make everything feel worse. But at the same time, I know avoiding them isn’t helping either. It’s like I’m caught between wanting to understand what I’m feeling and being afraid of what I might find. I don’t know if I’m ready for that yet.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely natural to feel scared about exploring those feelings, especially when they seem so intense. It sounds like you’re in a tough spot, wanting to understand your emotions but also feeling hesitant about what that might bring up. It’s okay to take your time with this. What do you think would help you feel a bit more ready or safe to explore those feelings when you decide to?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe if I had a bit more time to think about it or if I felt more comfortable talking about it, that would help. I think having some support, like knowing that it’s okay to feel what I’m feeling, would make a difference. It’s just hard to open up when I’m not sure how to put everything into words. If I could find a way to express it without feeling judged, maybe I’d feel a bit more ready to explore those feelings.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like having some time to reflect and feeling supported in your emotions would really help you feel more comfortable opening up. Knowing that it’s okay to feel what you’re feeling is important, and it’s understandable that finding the right words can be challenging. I want you to know that this is a safe space, and there’s no judgment here. When you’re ready, we can take it at your pace. Is there anything specific you think would help you feel more at ease in expressing those feelings?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think just having a little more time to gather my thoughts would help. Maybe if we could talk about it in smaller pieces, that might make it feel less overwhelming. I guess I just need to know that it’s okay to take things slow and that I don’t have to share everything all at once. It would be nice to feel like I’m not rushing into anything.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Absolutely, taking things slow and breaking it down into smaller pieces sounds like a great approach. It’s perfectly okay to share at your own pace, and there’s no need to rush into anything. We can explore whatever feels manageable for you. Is there a particular piece or aspect of what you’re feeling that you might want to start with, or would you prefer to just sit with those thoughts for now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’d like to start with just a small piece, maybe something about how I felt during the party. I remember feeling really anxious watching Ethan, and it was like I was just frozen, not knowing what to do. It’s hard to shake off that feeling of panic, like I should have acted but didn’t. Just thinking about it makes me feel tense again. I guess starting there feels a bit more manageable.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Starting with how you felt during the party sounds like a good step. It’s understandable that feeling anxious and frozen in that moment would create a lasting tension. That sense of panic and the belief that you should have acted can be really heavy to carry. Can you tell me a bit more about what that panic felt like for you in that moment? What thoughts were going through your mind?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: In that moment, it felt like my heart was racing, and I couldn’t really think straight. I kept telling myself that I needed to do something, but I just felt paralyzed. I remember thinking, “What if something really bad happens?” and “Why can’t I just help him?” It was like I was stuck between wanting to intervene and feeling like I didn’t know how. That panic made it hard to breathe, and I just felt so helpless. It’s frustrating to think back on it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds incredibly intense and overwhelming. Feeling your heart racing and struggling to think clearly in such a chaotic situation must have been really frightening. It’s understandable to feel frustrated when you wanted to help but felt paralyzed by panic. Those thoughts of “What if something really bad happens?” and “Why can’t I just help him?” reflect a deep sense of concern and responsibility. How do you feel about those thoughts now, looking back on that moment?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Looking back, I feel a mix of frustration and sadness. I wish I could have acted differently, but I also know that it was a really intense situation. It’s hard not to be hard on myself for not stepping in, but I guess I’m starting to realize that maybe I was just overwhelmed. It’s tough to accept that I couldn’t handle it the way I wanted to. I still feel that weight of responsibility, but I’m trying to remind myself that it’s okay to not have all the answers in those moments.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s understandable to feel that mix of frustration and sadness, especially when you wish you could have acted differently. Acknowledging that you were overwhelmed in such an intense situation is an important step. It sounds like you’re beginning to show yourself some compassion, recognizing that it’s okay not to have all the answers in those moments. That weight of responsibility can be heavy, but it’s good to hear you’re trying to remind yourself of your limits. What do you think might help you continue to cultivate that self-compassion moving forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think reminding myself that it’s okay to feel overwhelmed and that I’m not alone in these feelings would help. Maybe talking to friends about it more openly could also make a difference, even if it’s just to share that I’m struggling. I guess finding ways to be kinder to myself, like acknowledging that I did my best in a tough situation, could help too. It’s just a process, and I know it won’t happen overnight, but I want to keep working on it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have some thoughtful ideas about how to cultivate that self-compassion. Reminding yourself that it’s okay to feel overwhelmed and recognizing that you’re not alone can be really powerful. Talking to friends about your struggles could also provide support and connection. Acknowledging that you did your best in a tough situation is a kind and important step. It’s great that you’re aware this is a process and that you’re committed to working on it. What do you think your first step might be in putting some of these ideas into practice?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think my first step could be reaching out to a friend I trust and just sharing a bit about how I’ve been feeling lately. I don’t have to go into everything at once, but just opening up a little might help me feel less alone. I could also try to set aside some time for myself to reflect on what happened and remind myself that it’s okay to feel the way I do. Taking those small steps feels more manageable, and I think it could help me start to build that self-compassion.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a really positive plan. Reaching out to a trusted friend and sharing a bit about how you’ve been feeling can definitely help you feel less alone, and it’s a great way to start opening up. Setting aside time for yourself to reflect and remind yourself that your feelings are valid is also a wonderful step towards building that self-compassion. Taking those small, manageable steps can make a big difference. How do you feel about the idea of reaching out to your friend?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit nervous about it, to be honest. I worry about how they might react or if I’ll be able to express everything I want to say. But at the same time, I think it could be really helpful to share what I’m going through. I guess I just need to remind myself that it’s okay to be vulnerable and that my friends care about me. It’s a little scary, but I think it’s worth a shot.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely normal to feel nervous about reaching out and being vulnerable, especially when you’re unsure of how it will go. It’s great that you recognize the potential benefits of sharing with your friend, and reminding yourself that they care about you can help ease some of that anxiety. It’s okay to take your time with it, and even starting with just a small share can be a meaningful step. How do you think you might approach the conversation to help ease your nerves?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could start by just being honest about how I’ve been feeling lately without going into too much detail right away. Maybe I could say something like, “I’ve been dealing with some stuff that’s been weighing on me, and I could use someone to talk to.” That way, I’m not putting too much pressure on myself to explain everything all at once. I could also let them know that I’m just looking for support, not necessarily advice. Keeping it simple might help ease my nerves a bit.
